EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front draped over the region provided the focus for slow moving thunderstorm activity over northeastern Pennsylvania. Localized areas of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall contributed to some small stream and creek flooding near the Delaware River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported water, rocks and gravel running across Twin Lakes Road near the Handsome Eddy Road intersection due to culvert being blown out by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front advancing northward across the region resulted in several hours of dense fog across portions of eastern Pennsylvania during the early hours of March 2nd. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog were observed at Pocono Mountains Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain to portions of the Pocono Plateau. A glaze of ice up to a quarter of an inch and light sleet accumulation occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of freezing rain and sleet fell across the Pocono Plateau. Ice accretion up to a quarter of an inch and sleet accumulation up to one half inch occurred. The highest reported ice accretion was 0.20 inches in Mount Pocono. The highest sleet report was 0.3 inches in Mount Pocono.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of snow occurred from the afternoon of the 10th into the morning of the 11th with marginal temperatures. This yielded snowfall amounts that were highly elevation dependent across portions of eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coating of snow fell with event totals ranging from around 1 to 2 inches in the lower elevations to around 4 to 6 inches in the higher elevations near the Pocono Plateau. The highest reported snowfall total was 5.5 inches in Weatherly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow fell across portions of eastern Pennsylvania with marginal temperatures. Resulting snowfall amounts were highly elevation dependent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coating of snow fell across much of the county with the highest amounts occurring in the highest elevations. Totals generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches, although areas closer to the Pocono Plateau had closer to 7 inches. The highest reported total was 7.2 inches in Lake Harmony.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of patchy light freezing rain affected portions of central Pennsylvania during the pre-dawn hours of January 17, 2023, leading to isolated slick spots on area roads. Temperatures quickly rose above freezing once the sun came up, with patchy light rain continuing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain caused icy conditions and led to multiple vehicle accidents and the closure of the 1000 block of Route 879 near Pine Glen in Burnside Township, Centre County. No injuries were reported, and the road was reopened after being treated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter system brought freezing rain and some sleet to the Pocono Plateau region in Pennsylvania on the 22nd. Temperatures warmed above freezing by midnight, then a period of light snow resulted in some light snowfall accumulations in the morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and some sleet occurred across the Pocono Plateau region on the 22nd with a glaze of ice around one to two tenths of an inch. Temperatures warmed above freezing by midnight, then a period of light snow resulted in a coating of snow in the morning hours of the 23rd. The highest reported snowfall total was 2.5 inches in Pocono Pines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter system brought freezing rain and some sleet to the Pocono Plateau region in Pennsylvania on the 22nd. Temperatures warmed above freezing by midnight, then a period of light snow resulted in some light snowfall accumulations in the morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and some sleet occurred across the Pocono Plateau region on the 22nd with a glaze of ice around one tenth of an inch or less. Temperatures warmed above freezing by midnight, then a period of light snow resulted in some accumulating snowfall generally around one inch or less in the morning hours of the 23rd. However, isolated higher amounts occurred in the higher terrain. The highest reported total was 3.5 inches in Summit Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread accumulating snow near and north of the I-78 corridor as well as a light glaze of ice to the southern Poconos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from around 1 to 2 inches across much of Monroe County. The highest reported snowfall total was 2.5 inches at Pocono Summit. Additionally, a glaze of ice occurred from freezing rain across the Pocono Plateau. The KMPO ASOS reported 0.15 inches of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Occasional bands of lake-effect snow showers affected western Pennsylvania from January 26 into the early morning hours of January 27, 2023, putting down 2 to 3 inches of accumulation across the higher elevations of the Allegheny Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake-effect snow showers put down 2 to 3 inches of snow and slickened roads across Somerset County, resulting in two accidents and closing the eastbound lanes of the Pennsylvania Turnpike for 3 hours. The first accident involved a tractor trailer hitting the median barrier, and the second involved a plow truck being struck after it stopped to check on the first accident. The driver of the snow plow was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Occasional bands of lake-effect snow showers affected western Pennsylvania from January 26 into the early morning hours of January 27, 2023, putting down 2 to 3 inches of accumulation across the higher elevations of the Allegheny Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall impacted Blair and Huntingdon counties during the evening of January 26, 2023. Visibility is estimated to have dropped below half a mile, with winds gusting 20 to 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of patchy light freezing rain affected portions of central Pennsylvania during the pre-dawn hours of January 17, 2023, leading to isolated slick spots on area roads. Temperatures quickly rose above freezing once the sun came up, with patchy light rain continuing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain was enough to slicken roads across Tioga County, leading to several road closures due to icy conditions and vehicle accidents. Route 6 was closed from Mansfield to Route 549, Route 15 was closed from Route 287 to Mansfield, and Route 49 was closed in Lawrence Township from South Shore Lane to Erickson Road. Each of these roadways was closed for nearly an hour and a half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Ohio Valley into the eastern Great Lakes on January 25, 2023, pushing a swath of warm-advection snow across central Pennsylvania. The snow only last a few hours, with peak snowfall rates of 1-2 inches an hour. Most locations received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with some higher elevations spots receiving as much as 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of moderate to heavy snow caused slick roads and led to multiple accidents on I-81 northbound in Cumberland County. This caused the northbound lanes of the interstate to be closed between Exit 29 in Southampton Township and Exit 37 in Penn Township for over 3 hours. There were no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Eastern Seaboard from the Carolinas on January 22nd to the New England coast on the 23rd.  Southwest flow and jet ascent created widespread precipitation across the Upper Ohio Valley from the afternoon of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd.  Most of the snow accumulation was focused in areas north of I-70 in eastern Ohio and southwest Pennsylvania, save for some accumulations in the usual ridge locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.2 inches of snow was recorded at the NWS office in Moon, while between 2 and 3 inches were reported at Green Tree, Monroeville, and Wilkins Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread light precipitation to the region. A light coating of snow and glaze of ice from freezing rain and freezing fog occurred across the southern Poconos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of snow fell with total accumulations ranging from a half to one inch. Some sleet mixed in at times as well. The highest reported snow total was 1.0 inches in Pocono Summit. A glaze of ice also occurred from freezing rain and freezing fog once the snow and sleet ended, mainly across the Pocono Plateau. The KMPO ASOS reported 0.24 inches of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rode up the Middle Ohio Valley on January 25th. Ahead of the low, mid-level frontogenesis and a surface warm front combined to provide lift, providing morning wintry precipitation before warmer air changed most precipitation to rain by afternoon.  Most of multi-inch accumulation reports were found north of I-80 in Pennsylvania, as well as in portions of eastern Ohio. A trace of icing was also reported in a couple of locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In general, 2 to 3 inches was reported in the county. Cabot and Fox Run both reported high totals of 3.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front remained relatively stationary across the Upper Ohio Valley on January 3rd. An anomalously moist and warm air mass surged northward into the zone of convergence associated with the boundary, and combined with elevated instability to create enhanced rainfall rates.  In southwest Pennsylvania, Greene and Fayette Counties received the brunt of the rainfall, with storm total amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches in some cases. This rain led to some relatively minor road flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported on Iowa Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ripples of vorticity in a large-scale trough kept numerous snow showers falling across the Upper Ohio Valley on January 26th into the early morning hours of the 27th.  Steep low-level lapse rates and strong cold advection helped to briefly enhance snow rates at times. Most accumulations were in the 1 to 5 inch range, highest in the West Virginia ridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 2.6 inches was recorded at Marienville, and 2.3 inches fell at Tionesta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed a line of thunderstorms with strong winds through Pike County. Utility wires were knocked down at a couple of locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down utility wires along I-84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convective showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a passing cold front in Northeastern Pennsylvania. These storms became severe thanks to high shear being present on a cool February day. Strong winds knocked down trees and wires across the region. Some minor damage to personal property was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down 3 large power poles which resulted in numerous power outages in Powell and Weston areas. There was tree debris reported on several roads as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts were observed at a number of sites across the Ohio River Valley as a strong upper-level low crossed to our west and the Ohio River Valley resided with the warm sector. Sufficient mixing of dry air (surface temperatures in the 70s/dew points in the 30s) tapped into the low-level jet aloft (850mb winds of 45kt to 50kts) in the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Latrobe Airport measured a 46 mph wind gusts on the morning of February 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of April 5, 2023, accompanied by scattered showers and thunderstorms. A few gusty storms formed in the high shear/low-CAPE environment, and one storm produced minor wind damage in Somerset County. This activity weakened as is progressed eastward across central Pennsylvania and into a more stable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree west of Hooversville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of diurnal heating, strong subsidence, and a strong vertical wind profile with a crossing trough created strong wind gusts across the region on the afternoon of February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust of 47mph was measured at the Washington County Airport on the afternoon of February 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With clear conditions during the early morning hours of February 22nd prompting radiational cooling, dry air at the surface (dew points in the 20s), and a passing warm front, freezing rain was observed for a few hours at isolated areas as surface temperatures were near or just below freezing. Warm air advection and diurnal heating created icy conditions to cease by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain was observed the morning of February 22nd in portions of Allegheny County. About 0.02 inches of radial ice was estimated at Allegheny County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overrunning ahead of low pressure moving up the Ohio Valley produced a swath of light snow, sleet and freezing rain across much of central Pennsylvania during the morning and early afternoon of February 22, 2023. Although snow accumulations were generally 2 inches or less and ice accumulations were less than a quarter of an inch, it was enough to cause some travel issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light wintry mix caused slick conditions on US Route 15 in Jackson Township, Lycoming County, leading to a tractor trailer roll over in the median of the highway. This accident, and the subsequent hazmat clean-up, caused the road to be closed for more than three and a half hours. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought accumulating snow to Northeastern Pennsylvania on the night of the 27th. Snowfall was heavy for brief periods of time. While snow continued for much of the overnight hours, snow then transitioned to rain by the afternoon hours on the 28th. The highest snowfall totals were in the Poconos where up to 8 inches fell. Elsewhere, snowfall totals fell short of warning criteria but were enough to result in some minor impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of cold front, coupled with a strong low-level jet (850mb winds between 50 to 60kts,) created strong wind gusts in isolated areas across the Ohio River Valley the evening of February 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gusts across Allegheny County ranged between 40 to 50 mph; an isolated tree limb was blown down on Perry Street in Plum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of February 21, 2023, preceded by bands of gusty low-topped rain showers and thunderstorms, aided by steep lapse rates and appreciable deep-layer shear. Small (sub-severe) hail was also observed in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down multiple trees to the northwest of Bloomsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of February 21, 2023, preceded by bands of gusty low-topped rain showers and thunderstorms, aided by steep lapse rates and appreciable deep-layer shear. Small (sub-severe) hail was also observed in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sub-severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 50 mph turned over a pavilion, moved sheds, and spread playsets and debris across yards along Mulberry Street in Montoursville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of February 21, 2023, preceded by bands of gusty low-topped rain showers and thunderstorms, aided by steep lapse rates and appreciable deep-layer shear. Small (sub-severe) hail was also observed in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty rain showers producing winds estimated near 50 mph knocked out power to more than 250 customers in the borough of Blossburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of February 21, 2023, preceded by bands of gusty low-topped rain showers and thunderstorms, aided by steep lapse rates and appreciable deep-layer shear. Small (sub-severe) hail was also observed in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gusty rain shower produced winds estimated near 50 mph, causing the partial collapse of a garage that was under construction on Weikert Road a couple of miles east of the village of Weikert. One injury was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shield of light rain associated with warm advection pushed across central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of February 9, 2023. Temperatures were just cold enough in some locations north of I-80 for patchy light freezing rain at the onset of the precipitation. This produced just enough of an icy glaze in some locations for a handful of accidents across Tioga, Lycoming, and Sullivan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain produced just enough of an icy glaze for multiple accidents to occur on PA Route 42 in Davidson Township. The road was closed for just under an hour and a half while PennDOT treated it.

Warm air advection and diurnal heating created icy conditions to cease by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain was observed the morning of February 22nd in portions of Venango County. Sleet was also noted for a brief period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface low pressure tracked across Michigan and into eastern Canada on the 25th, along a track that is climatologically favored for strong wind events in the Upper Ohio Valley.  A crossing occluded boundary was a catalyst to start mixing strong wind to the surface.  Thunderstorms along this boundary helped to produce a few of the initial instances of wind damage to the north of Pittsburgh. A tight pressure gradient behind the front kept gusty wind going into the early evening. Widespread instances of 50 to 60 MPH wind gusts were reported, and many wind damage reports were received, especially from the more densely populated regions of Allegheny and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down in the area of Henlein.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening of April 15 produced locally heavy rain in eastern Pennsylvania.  Rainfall totals were as high as 2.0 to 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Fountain Hill became impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Jet-supported vertical motion and coincident frontogenesis combined to produce precipitation across southwest Pennsylvania during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th.  Antecedent warm conditions limited accumulations to areas near and north of the I-80 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A location east of Kennerdell received 2.2 inches of snow, with a few other accumulation reports of 1 to 2 inches noted in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the region on the 13th. Antecedent moisture and synoptic lift |created some convective snow showers with brief low visibility during the daytime hours, but a warm ground kept accumulations in check initially. Overnight, continued shortwave enhancement maintained snow over much of the region, and the cooling temperatures and ground surface allowed for some mainly minor accumulations.  A secondary shortwave trough continued some mainly light snow on the 14th before snow ended during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A system total of 2.2 inches was recorded at the Moon NWS office, with 2 inches or less reported across the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded surface low meandered near the eastern shore of Lake Superior during the late morning through early evening of the 12th. Simultaneously, the trailing cold front swept southeastward from southeastern Lower MI and northern IN to central Lake Erie and north-central/west-central portions of OH. Multiple multicell thunderstorms and a supercell thunderstorm occurred ahead of the cold front in northwest PA. These storms produced small hail, damaging convective gusts, and a tornado in a warm sector comprised of moderate to strong MUCAPE and effective bulk shear, respectively, mixed-layer lifting condensation levels near 750 to 1500 meters AGL, and strong surface-based effective storm-relative helicity near 150 meters squared per second squared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel-size in Spartansburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Top wind gust reports included 55 MPH at Allegheny County Airport, 53 MPH at a PADOT sensor on I-79 at Steubenville, and 52 MPH at Pittsburgh International Airport. One person was hospitalized when a tree fell on a car on the 800 block of North Lincoln Avenue in North Shore. Another car was damaged by a tree on 5th Avenue in East Mckeesport, with no injuries. Roof damage occurred to an apartment complex at Mount Lebanon, and siding was damaged on homes in Bon Meade. Several other reports of downed trees were received, including a report of one to two dozen downed in Stoop Ferry, which caught fire when a power pole was also involved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, an occluded low moved northeastward from southwestern Lower MI to central Lake Huron during the late morning through early evening of the 25th. Simultaneously, the accompanying warm front decelerated as it moved northeastward across northeast OH, into northwest PA, and toward the southwest corner of western New York. The low's trailing cold front swept eastward across northern OH and eventually overtook the warm front in northwest PA.||Clusters of multicell showers and thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts along the warm front and ahead of the cold front in northwest PA during the early to mid-afternoon. Moderate to strong effective bulk shear, weak MUCAPE, steep low-level lapse rates, and sizable DCAPE in the warm sector allowed precipitation associated with the surface-based and low-topped convection to be very efficient at transporting much stronger flow just aloft down to the surface. Some of the straight-line convective wind damage was associated with a brief microburst in Venango Township, Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed in Springfield Township (i.e. about six-miles west of Platea).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 3rd towards the northeast U.S. coast on the morning of the 4th. Ahead of the low, ideal conditions for enhanced downslope wind existed along west-facing slopes of Indiana, Westmoreland and Fayette Counties, as strong southeast flow underneath an inversion at ridgetop accelerated as it progressed downslope, creating many measured wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH on the 3rd.  Localized 70 MPH wind, although not measured, likely occurred as well.  Damage was most concentrated along the west-facing slopes of Fayette and Westmoreland, with many reports of tree and structural damage. Power outages were also widespread, with over 28% of Fayette County out at one point during the event. A Declaration of Disaster Emergency was issued for the county in the wake of the wind at noon on the 4th, with the city of Connellsville also issuing a local declaration.||Incidents of wind damage were more isolated in other portions of southwest Pennsylvania,  as wind gusts were slightly less severe, generally topping out at around 50 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered instances of wind damage included reports of single trees downed in Clarion and Knox, as well as a lifted and damaged carport in Fryburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought a coating of snow to portions of eastern Pennsylvania from the late evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coating of snow fell with event totals ranging from around 2 to 3 inches, however locally higher amounts as high as 7 inches fell in the higher elevations. The highest reported snowfall total was 7.0 inches in Lake Harmony.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado with estimated peak winds of 70 mph moved generally southeastward on the southeastern outskirts of Union City. The tornado began just northeast of the intersection of O'Neil Road and Concord Road, and ended before reaching Concord Road. The tornado damaged several trees. One of these trees was downed onto a home, which caused minor structural damage.

Ahead of the low, ideal conditions for enhanced downslope wind existed along west-facing slopes of Indiana, Westmoreland and Fayette Counties, as strong southeast flow underneath an inversion at ridgetop accelerated as it progressed downslope, creating many measured wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH on the 3rd.  Localized 70 MPH wind, although not measured, likely occurred as well.  Damage was most concentrated along the west-facing slopes of Fayette and Westmoreland, with many reports of tree and structural damage. Power outages were also widespread, with over 28% of Fayette County out at one point during the event. A Declaration of Disaster Emergency was issued for the county in the wake of the wind at noon on the 4th, with the city of Connellsville also issuing a local declaration.||Incidents of wind damage were more isolated in other portions of southwest Pennsylvania,  as wind gusts were slightly less severe, generally topping out at around 50 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 48MPH were reported at Washington County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought a coating of snow to portions of eastern Pennsylvania from the late evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coating of snow fell with event totals ranging from around 2 to4 inches, however higher amounts ranging from 5 to 6 inches fell across the Pocono Plateau. The highest reported snowfall total was 6.5 inches in Blakeslee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coating of snow fell with event totals ranging from around 1 to 3 inches, however higher amounts near 4 inches occurred in the northern most portion of the county. The highest reported snowfall total was 4.5 inches in Portland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of road closures due to downed trees and wires included State Route 259 and Ligonier Road near New Florence, Route 1007 near Conpitt Junction, Route 271 and Nature Run Road at Waterford, and Route 259 near Millbank. Numerous trees and power lines were also reported down in Ligonier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, an occluded low moved northeastward from southwestern Lower MI to central Lake Huron during the late morning through early evening of the 25th. Simultaneously, the accompanying warm front decelerated as it moved northeastward across northeast OH, into northwest PA, and toward the southwest corner of western New York. The low's trailing cold front swept eastward across northern OH and eventually overtook the warm front in northwest PA.||Clusters of multicell showers and thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts along the warm front and ahead of the cold front in northwest PA during the early to mid-afternoon. Moderate to strong effective bulk shear, weak MUCAPE, steep low-level lapse rates, and sizable DCAPE in the warm sector allowed precipitation associated with the surface-based and low-topped convection to be very efficient at transporting much stronger flow just aloft down to the surface. Some of the straight-line convective wind damage was associated with a brief microburst in Venango Township, Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel-size fell about three-miles WNW of Edinboro.

Ahead of the low, ideal conditions for enhanced downslope wind existed along west-facing slopes of Indiana, Westmoreland and Fayette Counties, as strong southeast flow underneath an inversion at ridgetop accelerated as it progressed downslope, creating many measured wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH on the 3rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of up to 56 MPH were recorded at Connellsville Airport.  Also near that town, a portion of a roof was blown off on Hyndman Street.  A gas station canopy was also blown over near Snydertown. Utility poles were downed on Route 119 in Bethelboro.  In Hopwood, shingles and siding were blown off of houses, with fence damage as well. Numerous trees and wires were blown down in the Uniontown area, with a tree also downed on a truck.  At the storm's peak, over 20,000 customers were without power in the county.  A Declaration of Disaster Emergency was issued for the county in the wake of the wind at noon on the 4th, with the city of Connellsville also issuing a local declaration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, an occluded low moved northeastward from southwestern Lower MI to central Lake Huron during the late morning through early evening of the 25th. Simultaneously, the accompanying warm front decelerated as it moved northeastward across northeast OH, into northwest PA, and toward the southwest corner of western New York. The low's trailing cold front swept eastward across northern OH and eventually overtook the warm front in northwest PA.||Clusters of multicell showers and thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts along the warm front and ahead of the cold front in northwest PA during the early to mid-afternoon. Moderate to strong effective bulk shear, weak MUCAPE, steep low-level lapse rates, and sizable DCAPE in the warm sector allowed precipitation associated with the surface-based and low-topped convection to be very efficient at transporting much stronger flow just aloft down to the surface. Some of the straight-line convective wind damage was associated with a brief microburst in Venango Township, Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm damage survey found evidence of a brief microburst in Venango Township. The maximum damage path width was 30 yards and estimated peak gusts were 100 mph. This microburst began one-mile north of Venango, just west of Plank Road, where numerous trees were snapped 10 to 30 feet above the ground. As the microburst advanced east-northeastward, it blew-out windows and tore off the sheet metal roof of a fishing camp, blew a tree onto a home, and blew debris onto an RV trailer. These structures were located between Plank Road and French Creek. Snapped trees and debris were strewn in the same direction, from WSW to ENE, indicative of straight-line convective wind damage associated with a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed ahead of an advancing cold front on the 23rd, providing a few instances of hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near the intersection of Brent Road and Jervis Road.

A strong cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening of April 1, 2023. In a high shear/low CAPE environment, convection was able to mix down very strong winds from just off the surface, leading to wind damage across portions of the area. Storms were weakening as they moved into central PA in the early afternoon, but they re-intensified over the Lower Susquehanna Valley by late afternoon and early evening. Winds following the frontal passage continued to gust to near 50 mph across southern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Route 6 east of Galeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms pushed into Pennsylvania from the south during the morning and afternoon of April 15, 2023, associated with upper-level low pressure approaching from the southern Appalachians. One storm that intensified over northern York County produced a brief funnel cloud. The funnel did not appear to touch down, and no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained NWS spotter observed a funnel cloud while traveling northbound on Route 15 near Clear Spring in northern York County. The funnel was observed near the intersection of York, Adams, and Cumberland counties. Pictures and video of the funnel show that it did not appear to touch down, and there was no reported damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed Pennsylvania during the afternoon of April 22, 2023, accompanied by showers and thunderstorms. In a low CAPE/moderate shear environment, the storms congealed into a north-south oriented line containing embedded bowing segments over the Susquehanna Valley and managed to produce sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph tore half of the roof off of the Areba Barn on the Hershey Medical Center campus. A large piece of the roofing landed on a car, denting the car and breaking the rear window. There were also several trees down in a residential area across Route 322 from the Barn, along Hillview Lane and West Areba Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of a closed upper-level low advanced just west of the Great Lakes on the evening of April 5th. An organized line of convection developed ahead of the cold front and created strong winds and isolated tornadoes from eastern Illinois to eastern Ohio. DCAPE values will likely near air above 1000J/kg with high cloud based ceilings prior to convection. Hi-Res model guidance also suggested that effective shear ranged between 30-40kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local 911 call center reported wires down on Route 168 and Hanover Kendall Road. Damage time was based on the radar reflectivity/velocity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed during the afternoon hours of April 20, 2023 in Rush Township, Centre County during a stretch of unseasonably warm and dry weather. By mid-morning on April 21, the fire had grown to approximately 1200 acres. At the fire's peak, two fixed wing air tankers and a helicopter were enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Scattered rain showers and cooler temperatures from the evening of April 21 into April 22 helped firefighters to gain control of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed during the afternoon of April 20, 2023 in Rush Township near the intersection of Sandy Ridge Trail and Ponderosa Drive. The wildfire occurred during a stretch of unseasonably warm and dry weather, and it quickly expanded in size to around 500 acres by evening. By mid-morning on April 21, the fire had grown to approximately 1200 acres. At the fire's peak, two fixed wing air tankers and a helicopter were enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Scattered rain showers and cooler temperatures from the evening of April 21 into April 22 helped firefighters to gain control of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed Pennsylvania during the afternoon of April 22, 2023, accompanied by showers and thunderstorms. In a low CAPE/moderate shear environment, the storms congealed into a north-south oriented line containing embedded bowing segments over the Susquehanna Valley and managed to produce sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a large branch along the 100 block of Sawmill Road in Providence Township, with one fatality reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening of April 1, 2023. In a high shear/low CAPE environment, convection was able to mix down very strong winds from just off the surface, leading to wind damage across portions of the area. Storms were weakening as they moved into central PA in the early afternoon, but they re-intensified over the Lower Susquehanna Valley by late afternoon and early evening. Winds following the frontal passage continued to gust to near 50 mph across southern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree across Route 66 south of Kane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm advection winter storm brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and some freezing rain to to region. The heaviest snowfall accumulations occurred in the southern Poconos with much of southeastern Pennsylvania receiving mainly rain.

The highest reported snow total was 7.5 inches in Mount Pocono. Some light freezing rain and sleet also mixed in as the precipitation was ending.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers developed along a strong cold front in western Ohio the morning of April 1st and advanced through eastern Ohio/western Pennsylvania. Widespread damage was recorded across the region. Gusts up 75mph were also reported in some areas. Despite little lightning in the line convection, the environment was favorable for strong wind gusts with CAPE values ranging between 100 to 400J/kg and a 50 to 65kt low-level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted at South Hills Village Mall..  Small hail was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching frontal boundary generated widely scattered showers and thunderstorms in a low-CAPE/high shear environment during the afternoon and early evening of March 25, 2023. These storms dissipated as they progressed eastward from western Pennsylvania into a more stable, cold air dammed airmass over central Pennsylvania. However, Warren County was able to break into the warm sector briefly, and a sub-severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 50 mph pushed across the northern part of the county and knocked down a tree onto wires. Following the frontal passage, strong winds up to 50 mph buffeted the western highlands and caused sporadic damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 50 mph knocked down a tree onto wires along PA Route 27 in Sugar Grove Township, closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley and passed over Pennsylvania March 3 into early on March 4, 2023. In the wake of this system, gusty west to northwest winds impacted the area. The winds were particularly strong across the Laurel Highlands and southern Alleghenies, where gusts approaching 60 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly winds occasionally gusting to near 60 mph occurred for several hours in Blair County. The ASOS at Altoona Airport recorded a wind gust to 58 mph, as did a mesonet site near Martinsburg. As a result, there were numerous reports of trees and wires down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving system tracked from the Ohio Valley eastward across southern Pennsylvania from March 6 into the morning of March 7. Strong warm advection and frontogenesis associated with this system helped to produce a narrow swath of 6 to 10 inches of snow across the northern mountains of Pennsylvania. Amounts decreased rapidly on either side of this narrow swath of heavier snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting winter storm produced a 8 to 10 inches of snow across all but far northern Potter County in less than 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked out of the Ohio Valley and through the southern Great Lakes on March 10th, passing just south of Erie, PA during the afternoon hours. A swath of accumulating snow occurred to the north of this low pressure, with a narrow band of heavy snow impacting extreme northwest Pennsylvania from late in the morning through much of the afternoon. Snow rates exceeded one inch per hour at times in this band of heavy snow. A trained spotter recorded a peak snowfall of 10.5 inches with this storm in North East Township. Elsewhere, a spotter measured 10 inches in Greene Township and 9.5 inches in Greenfield Township. Observers at Erie International Airport reported 6.1 inches of snow with this storm. Traffic accidents resulted, including a multi-vehicle accident along I-90 at mile marker 39 outside of North East Township. This accident closed I-90 Eastbound for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the evening of March 29, 2023, accompanied by a band of rain showers that quickly changed over to snow showers behind the front. Although roads were relatively warm to start, a quick drop in temperatures behind the front caused icy conditions to form in some areas. Wind gusts over 40 mph were observed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Post-frontal snow showers and a quick temperature drop led to patchy icy conditions on Centre County roadways on the evening of March 29, 2023. This, combined with winds gusting over 40 mph, led to a tractor trailer rollover on the 5500 block of Port Matilda Highway (US Route 322) westbound in Worth Township. The westbound lanes were closed for approximately two and a half hours as a result of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought accumulating snowfall to Northeastern Pennsylvania on the 10th and 11th. While snowfall totals were heaviest north and east of the region, accumulations of up to a half foot were still observed in Wayne County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm developed off the Mid-Atlantic Coast on March 13, 2023 and spread rain and snow northward into the eastern third of Pennsylvania from late March 13 into early March 14. Tioga County only received one to two inches of snow, but this was just enough to create slick roads that led to the closure of US Route 15 northbound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western edge of the precipitation shield from a coastal low produce 1 to 2 inches of snow across Tioga County on March 13-14, 2023. This snow was enough to produce slick conditions on roads across the county, and the northbound lanes of US Route 15 were close for just under an hour due to multiple tractor trailers becoming stuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into Northeastern Pennsylvania on the 13th. Snow continued throughout the day on the 14th and was heavy at times. Snow eventually came to an end on the 15th as the storm moved out of the region. Total snowfall across Northeastern Pennsylvania varied from just an inch to up to 9 inches in some locations, with elevation playing a factor in amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating helped the environment become unstable enough for isolated severe thunderstorms in portions of Northeastern Pennsylvania. There were a few reports of trees that were knocked down by the strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Ohio Valley and across Pennsylvania March 3 into early March 4, 2023. Snow, sleet, and patchy freezing rain overspread the area on March 3, before gradually changing over to light rain from southwest to northeast. 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet was observed from the central mountains into northeastern Pennsylvania. Any ice accretion was generally minimal and confined to higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow and sleet produced slick roads across Blair County during the afternoon of March 3, 2023. As a result, a box truck slid into the median of Old Route 22 in Allegheny Township, Blair County and produced a small diesel fuel spill. The road was closed briefly so that tow trucks could remove the box truck.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch or two of wet snow and sleet led to slick roads across Schuylkill County on March 3, 2023, resulting in an accident involving a tractor Trailer on I-81 southbound on the bridge over Mollystown Road in Pine Grove Township. The trailer ended up hanging off the bridge over Mollystown Road, causing a hazard to the road below. There was a lane restriction on I-81 southbound for nearly 6 hours while the truck was removed and the bridge was checked for structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved east-northeastward from southern Lake Michigan to Lake Ontario during the morning through early afternoon of April 1st. Simultaneously, the low's trailing cold front moved from the southwestern Great Lakes and Middle MS Valley toward central NY and the Upper OH Valley. Scattered multicell showers and thunderstorms persisted generally northeastward and eastward ahead of, along, and just behind the surface cold front during the wee hours of the 1st and again during the early afternoon of the 1st. These showers and storms encountered moderate to strong effective bulk shear, weak DCAPE, and weak MUCAPE that increased somewhat and transitioned from elevated to surface-based by late morning as the boundary layer destabilized via daytime heating. Despite the marginally-favorable thermodynamic environment, showers and storms generated sporadic damaging convective gusts during the wee hours of the 1st and again during the early afternoon of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Meadville Airport ASOS recorded a peak gust of 58 mph from passing thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southwestward from New England into Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of June 3, 2023, spawning scattered thunderstorms. SBCAPE was moderately high (>2000 J/kg), and deep layer (0-6 km) shear of 40 kts was supportive of supercells across the eastern half of Pennsylvania. Perhaps the most notable aspects of this event were the large hail and the northeast-southwest cell movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near East Buffalo Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 1:30 PM on June 12, 2023, an EF0 tornado touched down in Conewago Township, York County. The tornado crossed Cloverleaf Road and tracked northeastward for just over one-third of a mile before lifting around 1:32 PM. A few trees were uprooted between Cloverleaf Road and Kern Road. Maximum winds were estimated to be around 75 mph, and the maximum path width was approximately 130 yds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1:30 PM on June 12, 2023, an EF0 tornado touched down in Conewago Township, York County. The tornado crossed Cloverleaf Road and tracked northeastward for just over one-third of a mile before lifting around 1:32 PM. A few trees were uprooted between Cloverleaf Road and Kern Road. Maximum winds were estimated to be around 75 mph, and the maximum path width was approximately 130 yds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered showers and storms developed over central and eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of June 6, 2023. The boundary layer was relatively dry, with dewpoints in the 40s to low 50s, and there was only modest shear. However, steep mid-level lapse rates did help to produce some modest CAPE (500 J/kg). A couple of the storms that developed produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph produced multiple reports of trees down in Newberry, Conewago, and Manchester townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the East Coast and into Quebec on December 18th. Behind this low pressure, wrap-around moisture and a series of troughs set off light snow across the region from December 18th into early December 19th. At the same time cold northwest winds picked up moisture off of Lake Erie, leading to moderate to heavy lake enhanced snow southeast of the lake in parts of Northwest Pennsylvania. The heaviest snow occurred in the higher elevations southeast of Lake Erie between the evening hours of December 18th and the early morning hours of December 19th. Snow rates exceeded 1��� per hour at times with storm total amounts reaching 7 to nearly 13 inches in the higher elevations in parts of Erie and Crawford Counties. A trained spotter measured 12.9��� of snow in Waterford, the highest amount from the storm. Other select totals include trained spotters measuring 12��� in Venango, 11.6��� in Edinboro and 10.5��� in Cambridge Springs. The moderate to heavy snow was accompanied by wind gusts of 30 to 40 MPH, leading to reduced visibility at times. Roads were snow covered and slick and accidents resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the East Coast and into Quebec on December 18th. Behind this low pressure, wrap-around moisture and a series of troughs set off light snow across the region from December 18th into early December 19th. At the same time cold northwest winds picked up moisture off of Lake Erie, leading to moderate to heavy lake enhanced snow into Erie County, especially south of I-90. The heaviest snow occurred between the evening hours of December 18th and the early morning hours of December 19th. Snow rates exceeded 1��� per hour at times. A trained spotter measured 12.9��� of snow in Waterford, the highest amount from the storm. Other selected totals from trained spotters include 11.6��� in Edinboro, 9.3��� in Colt Station and 8.5��� in Union City. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry measured 8���. The moderate to heavy snow was accompanied by wind gusts of 30 to 40 MPH, leading to reduced visibility at times. Roads were snow covered and slick and accidents resulted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent system moved through eastern Pennsylvania bringing damaging winds, large hail, and a tornado with scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado began in West Caln Township near Baldwin Road where a hardwood tree was uprooted and found hanging on top of downed wires. South of here, between Baldwin Road and Chantry Lane, another hardwood tree was found snapped at its trunk. This was the area that the tornado reached its maximum width of approximately 250 yards. The tornado then continued eastward with tree and wire damage seen on North Sandy Hill Road near Wallace Lane where a tree and large limbs fell on and damaged a wooden fence. Continuing further east, a row of hardwood trees was severely damaged with snapped trunks on Martins Corner Road just north of Wagontown Road. A utility pole was also snapped here, but this appeared to have been caused by the falling trees and the strain introduced to the wires since the pole may have been weakened by age. The tornado then dissipated as there was no concentrated damage found east of Martins Corner Road. Additional damage separate from this path was noted and appeared to be caused by straight line winds. This includes tree damage in the Hidden Acres Campground on the northwest side of Baldwin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest PA resided in a warm and very humid air mass during the late morning through early evening of the 6th as a cold front approached slowly from the western Great Lakes. A line of multicell thunderstorms persisted eastward along a pre-front surface trough axis and generated localized straight-line convective wind damage in the Erie area as the storms encountered moderate MUCAPE, DCAPE, and effective bulk shear, respectively, in northwest PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed along Hershey Road, near I-79, in Erie, PA area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front stalled over the region early on April 6th, resulting in light winds with a moist air mass and warm air moving over it aloft. This resulted in ideal conditions for fog development on the cool side of the front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front ignited a broken line of storms across portions of the county warning area. Storms remained pretty tame through 23Z before an increase of wind shear from the west, associated with a closed upper-level low, overspread western PA. Damaging wind gusts were mainly focused east of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 30 to 40 trees down along River Forest Road and River Forest Country Club. Power lines also done. Likely microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region, with a line of strong showers and thunderstorms developing out ahead of the front. Storms produced damaging winds, tornadoes, and small to medium-sized hail in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed as a line of storms moved through lower Bucks County. Several trees were uprooted and snapped on the south side of Wrightstown Township. As the tornado progressed southeast near Swamp road, additional tree damage occurred in residential areas as well as on the Bucks County Community College Campus. The |maximum estimated wind speed was associated with damage observed on the north side of Newtown where a roof facade was blown over on a portion of a strip mall building. Additional tree damage was observed in downtown Newtown. The continuous path of damage ended in the vicinity of the Newtown Cemetery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front the afternoon of June 3rd. With mid-level dry air prompting high DCAPE values (over 1000J/kg), damaging wind gusts were the main concern. However, hail the size of quarters fell in a few isolated storms due to higher terrain prompting a lower freezing level and a few storms mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manger reported State Route 19 closed due to trees and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms ahead of a strong cold front developed and moved through the region. Storms produced gusty winds, small to medium sized hail, and a tornado in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado began less than a mile southwest of Womelsdorf along PA Route 419 between Memorial Boulevard and Brickplant Road just over the Berks and Lebanon County line where a few old wooden telephone poles were pushed over. The tornado continued northeast through a field toward the Conrad Weiser West Elementary School. A few softwood trees in a tree line were snapped or uprooted along the property line of the field and the school. This is the location where the tornado reached its maximum estimated winds of 90 mph and maximum width of approximately 125 yards. The tornado continued across the school grounds and into a recreational field where a set |of metal bleachers was blown over. The tornado then moved northeast into the central part of Womelsdorf where varying degrees of minor damage occurred to structures and trees, mainly between 3rd Street and 2nd Street as the tornado crossed Cherry Alley, High Street, Mulberry Alley, and Franklin Street. Several homes, garages, and |barns had roofing material blow off, several small trees were uprooted, and numerous downed large tree branches caused some collateral structural damage to some homes and vehicles. The tornadic circulation began to weaken considerably as it crossed 2nd |Street north of Franklin Street near Pear Alley where some additional minor tree damage occurred. The tornado dissipated as it moved into the Womelsdorf Union Cemetery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and storms pushed into south-central Pennsylvania from the southwest during the evening of July 15, 2023. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line pushed from the Great Lakes into central Pennsylvania during the evening of July 20, 2023. An intense storm formed just ahead of the squall line over Warren County producing damaging winds and large hail. Then the squall line arrived with more widespread damaging winds as it crossed central Pennsylvania from northwest to southeast through the late evening and early overnight hours.

Additional showers and storms developed just north of the track of this initial cluster of storms, coagulating into a line that pushed northeastward across the central mountains during the evening hours producing sporadic wind damage in Centre County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred on the north side of Lewisburg in Union County, just south of the Kelly Township Fields. The tornado moved ENE and then NE until it dissipated just west of Milton Area High/Middle School. The tornado was rated an EF-1, with a path length of nearly 2.5 miles and a max path width of approximately 150 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred on the north side of Lewisburg in Union County, just south of the Kelly Township Fields. The tornado moved ENE snapping trees before tipping over|an RV in a Flea Market, along with blowing away an open structure. The tornado deviated to more of a northeasterly track as it tore off the metal roof of a fireworks store, causing damage to cars parked at the business and launching pieces of the metal roof across U.S. Highway 15. More sporadic damage was then observed across U.S. Highway 15 and the Susquehanna River in western portions of Northumberland County. Several small trees were snapped off along South Drive with periodic damage observed along roads intersecting Shakespeare Avenue, with the most notable being areas of a roof being torn off of a residence at the intersection of Jordan Street and Wood Street. The tornado continued to show sporadic damage until it dissipated slightly west of Milton Area High/Middle School at 4:33 PM, where insulation continued to be observed from the Flea Market on the north side of Lewisburg. The tornado was rated an EF-1, with a path length of nearly 2.5 miles and a max path width of approximately 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Lewis Township during the afternoon of July 2, 2023. The tornado touched down just south of Route 45 to the east of Hartelon and tracked ENE before dissipating just north of Kaiser Run Road. The rating was EF-1, path length was around 1.5 miles, and max path width was approximately 150 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Lewis Township during the afternoon of July 2, 2023. The  tornado touched down just south of Route 45 to the east of Harleton and tracked ENE causing damage to a well-constructed chicken coop along with uplifting a majority of a barn`s roof and spreading it throughout a nearby field. The tornado tracked just west of a residence along Pleasant Grove Road, where many large limbs were observed to be down with a healthy cherry tree snapped before continuing to move northeastward. Trees along the intersection of Kaiser Run Road and Windy Valley Lane were uprooted and snapped with a small outer building having damage lofted into a nearby field. The tornado dissipated just north of Kaiser Run Road. The rating was EF-1, path length was around 1.5 miles, and max path width was approximately 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest Pennsylvania remained in the warm sector during the late morning through early evening hours of July 6th as a cold front drifted eastward across far-western Lake Erie and western OH. A cluster of multicell showers and thunderstorms well-ahead of the front persisted eastward in Crawford County, PA during the late afternoon and produced isolated straight-line convective wind damage. These showers and storms encountered moderate MUCAPE and effective bulk shear, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed in Hayfield Township, about three-miles west of Saegertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado occurred near Route 147 in Upper Paxton Township, Dauphin County. The tornado was rated an EF-0, with a path length of just under 1 mile and a max path width of approximately 50 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado occurred near Route 147 in Upper Paxton Township, Dauphin County. Trees were downed by the tornado, blocking the roadway at 3462 PA 147. The tornado damage was intermittent as it traveled east for about 1 mile, downing more trees as it crossed over Shippen Dam Rd. The survey also found a larger area of more sporadic, non-tornadic wind damage to the north of the tornado track, which extended nearly 20 miles from Seven Stars in Juniata County to Gratz in Dauphin County. This damage mostly consisted of downed trees and tree limbs, as well as downed|powerlines on Specktown Road in Lykens Twp, Dauphin County. It was determined that this larger area of sporadic damage was associated with the forward flank downdraft of the same storm, which is an area of straight line winds within a supercell. The tornado was rated an EF-0, with a path length of just under 1 mile and a max path width of approximately 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of eastern Pennsylvania from the late afternoon into the evening of July 15.  Rainfall totals were as high as 3 to 6 inches.  Catastrophic flash flooding occurred in Upper Makefield Township in Bucks County, where several lives were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in Lower Makefield Township.  There were numerous road closures and water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed across parts of SE PA on July 10th with a few thunderstorms turning severe, producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a widespread area of somewhat sporadic straight line wind damage. A survey team found damage beginning in northeast portion of East Rockhill Township, where a tree fell onto a house on East Rock Road. The damaging winds continued in an east to southeast swath into the southwest portion of Bedminster Township. The survey team noted numerous uprooted trees, downed limbs and branches in this area. Several of these fell onto powerlines, causing powerlines and some power poles to fall. There were a couple of snapped trees, however these were weakened by interior rotting of the trunks. There were dozens of corn fields that had leaning or flattened corn stalks in this area as well. As the strong winds continued southeast into Plumstead Township, the damage became noticeably more sporadic and generally less severe in nature. A few more trees were uprooted, some falling onto powerlines. The last large tree that was found uprooted was on Curly Hill Road, where it also downed powerlines and a few power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, a low drifted east-southeastward across southern Lower MI and toward the northwest OH border during the late evening of August 24th through wee hours of the 25th. Simultaneously, the warm front preceding the low moved northward and northeastward across southern ON and toward Lake Ontario, while the trailing cold front moved south-southeastward to northern IN and IL, and southern IA and NE. A warm and humid sector comprised of moderate to strong MUCAPE and moderate effective bulk shear resided across northwest PA. Ahead of the low and trailing cold front, a line of multicell thunderstorms persisted east-southeastward across northwest PA during the wee hours of August 25th and produced localized straight-line convective wind damage in southwestern Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed in the Pymatuning South area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of July 2, 2023, as a disturbance tracked eastward along a frontal boundary stalled over southern New York state. In an environment with relatively modest buoyancy (1000-1500 J/kg CAPE) and strong shear (0-1 km SRH of approximately 200 m2/s2). Discrete cells were able to develop on the southern periphery of this activity. Several of these cells produced wind damage, and there were also five tornadoes from the central mountains eastward into the middle Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed by a trained spotter in the vicinity of Millerstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms developed over south-central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of July 28, 2023, just south of a frontal boundary that was draped from northwest to southeast across Pennsylvania. This cluster of storms produced sporadic wind damage as it progressed eastward across the Cumberland and Lower Susquehanna valleys. Additional showers and storms developed just north of the track of this initial cluster of storms, coagulating into a line that pushed northeastward across the central mountains during the evening hours producing sporadic wind damage in Centre County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph snapped more than a dozen trees, damaging one car and five outbuildings. Additionally, a large field of corn was knocked over. Radar image shows an outflow boundary as primary component responsible for the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm impacted the Laurel Highlands and localized flooding impacted the city of Altoona during the afternoon of July 1, 2023. Scattered thunderstorm developed in a modest CAPE environment (1000-1500 J/kg) and modest shear environment ahead of an approaching mid-level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph blew off a section of metal roof and knocked down power lines. Debris was blown across Route 281 near Friedens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of July 2, 2023, as a disturbance tracked eastward along a frontal boundary stalled over southern New York state. In an environment with relatively modest buoyancy (1000-1500 J/kg CAPE) and strong shear (0-1 km SRH of approximately 200 m2/s2). Discrete cells were able to develop on the southern periphery of this activity. Several of these cells produced wind damage, and there were also five tornadoes from the central mountains eastward into the middle Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph produced multiple reports of trees down across Upper Paxton and Mifflin townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain in eastern Pennsylvania on July 16.  Rainfall totals were as high as 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters reached the first floor of a house in Washington Township, causing a basement wall to collapse.  A bridge on Lower South Main Street in the township was severely damaged due to rushing flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of eastern Pennsylvania from the late afternoon into the evening of July 15.  Rainfall totals were as high as 3 to 6 inches.  Catastrophic flash flooding occurred in Upper Makefield Township in Bucks County, where several lives were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Catastrophic flash flooding occurred in Upper Makefield Township, resulting in the loss of seven lives.  A reliable rain gauge located within the township indicated that 4.64 inches of rain fell during the event which lasted less than 2 hours.  Widespread roadway flooding occurred with many roads becoming impassable.  Numerous water rescues took place in the township.  The worst of the flooding occurred along Hough's Creek.  The creek rose rapidly and the rushing water carried away many vehicles, most along Taylorsville Road which parallels the Delaware Canal.  Eleven vehicles were reported to have been swept into the creek.  Eight individual were rescued from their vehicle and two from the creek.  Unfortunately, seven people perished either while trapped in their vehicle or while attempting to escape the flood waters.||The Taylorsville Road Bridge over Hough's Creek was structurally compromised.  PA Route 532 between Stonebridge Crossing Road and Wrightstown Road was damaged severely by the raging flood waters from Hough's Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred to the southeast of Zion on the afternoon of July 2, 2023. The tornado occurred just east of the end of Ralph's Lane and moved east-northeastward, before dissipating east of Dusty Lane. No structures were directly impacted by the tornado, as it moved through a predominantly forested area. The tornado was rated EF-1, the path length was less than one mile, and the path width was approximately 200 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred to the southeast of Zion on the afternoon of July 2, 2023. The tornado touched down just east of the end of Ralph's Lane and moved east-northeastward, uprooting several large trees. The tornado dissipated east of Dusty Lane. No structures were directly impacted by the tornado, as it moved through a predominantly forested area. The tornado was rated EF-1, the path length was less than one mile, and the path width was approximately 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred in West Buffalo Township during the afternoon of July 2, 2023. Initial touchdown was in a field just west of Pleasant Grove Road, and the tornado tracked northeastward towards the intersection of Church Road and Clark Avenue before dissipating just south of a bend in Church Road. The tornado was rated an EF-1, with a path length of just over 2 miles and a max path width of approximately 50 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in West Buffalo Township during the afternoon of July 2, 2023. Initial touchdown was in a field just west of Pleasant Grove Road, and the tornado tracked adjacent to Dietrich Road towards Pine Valley Trailer Park where some minor damage was observed to outer buildings. The tornado continued northeastward towards the intersection of Church Road and Clark Avenue where garage door collapsed inward and a roof was lifted off. The tornado dissipated just south of a bend in Church Road. The tornado was rated an EF-1, with a path length of just over 2 miles and a max path width of approximately 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass over central Pennsylvania with moderate CAPE and very little CIN ahead of an approaching cold front. A few of the storms produced sporadic wind damage, and the widespread nature of this activity allowed the event to evolve into a flash flooding threat as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a downburst just north of Liverpool on the morning of July 9, with winds estimated between 85 and 95 mph. Dozens of trees were uprooted or snapped, and areas of knee-high corn were disrupted in this rural part of Perry County, PA. The path length of the downburst was less than a quarter of a mile, with a maximum path width of approximately 125 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief-lived EF0 tornado down in a corn field south of the 1000 block of Truce Road near Rawlinsville during the early evening of August 7, 2023. The tornado pushed northeastward, briefly lifting and then touching back down. The total path length was approximately half a mile, with a maximum estimated peak wind around 85 mph and a maximum path width of 20 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado down in a corn field south of the 1000 block of Truce Road near Rawlinsville during the early evening of August 7, 2023. Arial imagery reveals a narrow|swath of corn downed in a convergent pattern along the tornado track as it moved toward the northeast. On the north side of Truce Road, the tornado snapped the trunk of one large tree and broke limbs off of a few others. The tornado then lifted briefly before touching back down in a second cornfield, downing another swath of corn in a convergent pattern.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved east-southeastward from northwestern Lower MI to the southeastern shore of Lake Huron during the late morning through mid-evening of the 20th. Simultaneously, the low's warm front swept northeastward across northwestern PA to central Lake Ontario and central NY, and the low's trailing cold front moved generally eastward from northwestern Lower MI and southern Lake Michigan to southern Lake Huron and near the western shore of Lake Erie. Moderate to strong MUCAPE and effective bulk shear, respectively, were present just ahead of the surface warm front and within the warm sector.||A multicell thunderstorm in vicinity of the surface warm front persisted northeastward across northern portions of Erie County, including the city of Erie, during the early afternoon and produced severe hail and damaging convective wind gusts. A semi-discrete supercell then moved generally eastward across southern Erie County during the late afternoon through early evening and produced severe hail, including large hail of 2-inches in diameter in the Corry area. Following the supercell, a line of multicell thunderstorms persisted generally eastward and southeastward across northwest Pennsylvania and produced isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Conneautville, Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up quarter-size about one-mile west of Union City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly southward-moving front was draped across central Ohio and Pennsylvania on July 13th.  Warm air aloft initially kept storm development muted, but a mesoscaled convective vortex fired thunderstorms by early to mid-afternoon over the Middle Ohio Valley.  These storms lifted northeastward into an environment characterized by 40 to 50 knots of deep-layer shear and up to 2500 J/kg of mixed layer CAPE.  Dry air aloft also enhanced the downburst wind threat.  As a result, many reports of tree damage were received across southwest Pennsylvania, along with several instances of marginally severe hail.

The passage of a warm front during the morning of July 20th provided a moist and unstable air mass.  A strong, progressive upper trough tracked southeastward from the Great Lakes, with an associated cold front crossing during the evening hours. Mixed-layer CAPE of up to 2500 J/kg, strong deep shear, and steep low and mid-level lapse rates were supportive of severe weather.  Widespread reports of wind damage were received, mainly to trees.  Some isolated urban flash flooding also occurred in Beaver and Allegheny Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported to have entered a basement in Greenville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Clairton Boulevard and North Lewis Run Road was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a warm front during the morning of July 20th provided a moist and unstable air mass.  A strong, progressive upper trough tracked southeastward from the Great Lakes, with an associated cold front crossing during the evening hours. Mixed-layer CAPE of up to 2500 J/kg, strong deep shear, and steep low and mid-level lapse rates were supportive of severe weather.  Widespread reports of wind damage were received, mainly to trees.  Some isolated urban flash flooding also occurred in Beaver and Allegheny Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five vehicles were reported to be stuck in flood water on the ramp to the McKeesport-Duquesne Bridge, necessitating the activation of a swift water rescue team.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly southward-moving front was draped across central Ohio and Pennsylvania on July 13th.  Warm air aloft initially kept storm development muted, but a mesoscaled convective vortex fired thunderstorms by early to mid-afternoon over the Middle Ohio Valley.  These storms lifted northeastward into an environment characterized by 40 to 50 knots of deep-layer shear and up to 2500 J/kg of mixed layer CAPE.  Dry air aloft also enhanced the downburst wind threat.  As a result, many reports of tree damage were received across southwest Pennsylvania, along with several instances of marginally severe hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved east-southeastward from northwestern Lower MI to the southeastern shore of Lake Huron during the late morning through mid-evening of the 20th. Simultaneously, the low's warm front swept northeastward across northwestern PA to central Lake Ontario and central NY, and the low's trailing cold front moved generally eastward from northwestern Lower MI and southern Lake Michigan to southern Lake Huron and near the western shore of Lake Erie. Moderate to strong MUCAPE and effective bulk shear, respectively, were present just ahead of the surface warm front and within the warm sector.||A multicell thunderstorm in vicinity of the surface warm front persisted northeastward across northern portions of Erie County, including the city of Erie, during the early afternoon and produced severe hail and damaging convective wind gusts. A semi-discrete supercell then moved generally eastward across southern Erie County during the late afternoon through early evening and produced severe hail, including large hail of 2-inches in diameter in the Corry area. Following the supercell, a line of multicell thunderstorms persisted generally eastward and southeastward across northwest Pennsylvania and produced isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Conneautville, Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size in Erie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain during the morning of July 9th helped to saturate the ground, and additional thunderstorms in an anomalously high moisture environment during the early evening hours created flash flooding.  Wayne and Shenango Townships in Lawrence County were particularly hard hit, with at least eight homes in these areas impacted by flooding. Many roads were also impacted. An isolated instance of wind damage was also noted in Beaver County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly southward-moving front was draped across central Ohio and Pennsylvania on July 13th.  Warm air aloft initially kept storm development muted, but a mesoscaled convective vortex fired thunderstorms by early to mid-afternoon over the Middle Ohio Valley.  These storms lifted northeastward into an environment characterized by 40 to 50 knots of deep-layer shear and up to 2500 J/kg of mixed layer CAPE.  Dry air aloft also enhanced the downburst wind threat.  As a result, many reports of tree damage were received across southwest Pennsylvania, along with several instances of marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report and photo of trees snapped in half in Bell Township were received. Additionally, there was a report of damage to outdoor window shutters and a roof vent was pulled up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain during the morning of July 9th helped to saturate the ground, and additional thunderstorms in an anomalously high moisture environment during the early evening hours created flash flooding.  Wayne and Shenango Townships in Lawrence County were particularly hard hit, with at least eight homes in these areas impacted by flooding. Many roads were also impacted. An isolated instance of wind damage was also noted in Beaver County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Union Valley Road at Old Pittsburgh Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved east-southeastward from northwestern Lower MI to the southeastern shore of Lake Huron during the late morning through mid-evening of the 20th. Simultaneously, the low's warm front swept northeastward across northwestern PA to central Lake Ontario and central NY, and the low's trailing cold front moved generally eastward from northwestern Lower MI and southern Lake Michigan to southern Lake Huron and near the western shore of Lake Erie. Moderate to strong MUCAPE and effective bulk shear, respectively, were present just ahead of the surface warm front and within the warm sector.||A multicell thunderstorm in vicinity of the surface warm front persisted northeastward across northern portions of Erie County, including the city of Erie, during the early afternoon and produced severe hail and damaging convective wind gusts. A semi-discrete supercell then moved generally eastward across southern Erie County during the late afternoon through early evening and produced severe hail, including large hail of 2-inches in diameter in the Corry area. Following the supercell, a line of multicell thunderstorms persisted generally eastward and southeastward across northwest Pennsylvania and produced isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Conneautville, Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees downed about three-miles ENE of downtown Erie.
